meta.description: Australian candidate transparency dataset: the federal 48th Parliament plus state/territory parliaments (see jurisdiction field). Federal roster from AEC Members Elected (House) and OpenAustralia (Senate) with AEC donor data; state rosters from data/sources/states/. Policy positions are added separately with their own sources. See ../CONTRIBUTING.md.

immigration.summary: Payne criticises Australia's immigration detention as 'destructive and expensive' and argues indefinite detention is unjustifiable. She supports abolishing temporary protection visas, expanding the government-funded humanitarian intake (citing Labor's 27,000-place commitment) plus a 5,000-place community-sponsored program, increasing UNHCR funding, and accepting New Zealand's offer to resettle refugees.

sources.title: Refugees - 23/06/2021

foreign_policy.summary: In a 28 July 2025 interview, Payne said she has continually called for an immediate and permanent ceasefire in Gaza as the most effective way to protect innocent lives, stated that by not allowing aid into Gaza Israel is breaching international law, and said she longs to see Australia recognise a Palestinian state as soon as possible, at a time most advantageous to peace.

sources.title: Afternoon Briefing Panel 28th July 2025

faith.summary: Andrew Leigh is a self-described atheist; he has written and spoken publicly about being an atheist while arguing that churches benefit society.

sources.title: The Atheist Who Wants More Churches in Australia
The church is good for social connection, says federal politician and atheist

immigration.summary: Leigh, a Labor MP and economist, publicly defends immigration, stating in 2026 that the government has 'reduced migration from the record highs it was under the Coalition.' He has more broadly framed Australia's non-discriminatory and skilled migration system as a source of national strength.

sources.title: Andrew Leigh can't stop lying on immigration

foreign_policy.summary: Leigh has advocated for evidence-based foreign aid, stating the Australian Government understands its obligation to ensure aid delivered has the maximum positive impact and arguing that randomised trials and rigorous evaluation should determine which development interventions work.

sources.title: The call for evidence-based development

economic_nationalism.summary: Andrew Leigh is a strong advocate for free trade and unilateral tariff reduction, opposing protectionism. He has argued Australia should continue advocating for tariff removal without imposing retaliatory tariffs, stating "Australia is a trading nation and a strong advocate for open and rules-based trade" and "Trade expands opportunity. Isolation shrinks it."

sources.title: Leigh takes a swipe at US tariffs with passionate speech in defence of free trade

faith.summary: Alex Hawke was raised as an Anglican but now attends Hillsong Church; in his maiden speech he affirmed Christian values such as grace, forgiveness and redemption.

sources.title: Wikipedia — Alex Hawke

immigration.summary: Hawke, a former Liberal Immigration Minister, has called for the government to resolve the status of roughly 7,500 long-term asylum seekers, saying: 'I think the Government should resolve this now. People have been here a long time and the reality of their condition is that they will be staying here.'

sources.title: RCOA welcomes Liberal ex-Minister's call to end years of waiting

foreign_policy.summary: As Minister for International Development and the Pacific, Hawke called for closer ties to and development assistance for the Pacific region, stating 'their prosperity is our prosperity,' while supporting a reduction in the overall foreign aid budget. He also argued his Pacific engagement did not require further action by Australia on climate change.

sources.title: Alex Hawke

economic_nationalism.summary: In a 2019 keynote, Alex Hawke referenced the Coalition government's legislation "establishing the kind of rules around foreign investment in critical national infrastructure" and "banning foreign donations into the Australian political system," stating this legislation "has really shored up the ability of Australia to stand strong" and that "a strong, secure sovereign region fundamentally enables economic prosperity."

sources.title: Australia's Focus on the Pacific - Hon Alex Hawke MP's Keynote Speech to the AIIA National Conference

citizenship_eligibility.summary: During the 2017 citizenship questions over members with foreign-born parents, Hawke stated he is 'an Australian citizen only and have never held or acquired or sought Greek or any other citizenship.' He was not referred to the High Court.

sources.title: I'm not a Greek citizen: Liberal MP Alex Hawke

immigration.summary: Gee, an independent MP, championed the case of the Bathurst-based Nguyen family to remain in Australia, welcoming the Immigration Minister's grant of visas after a petition of over 22,000 signatures, saying it 'brings to an end a decade of stress and uncertainty for this much-loved Bathurst family' and was 'proof that people power can prevail.'

sources.title: Victory for People Power! The Nguyen Family Can Stay! Minister Approves Visas!

economic_nationalism.summary: In his maiden speech, Andrew Gee called for "a targeted, national manufacturing strategy," arguing "Other countries with high labour costs have been able to do it and we can too," and advocated value-adding to agriculture, noting "the vast majority of agricultural commodities produced in the central west leave the region without any value adding" and that food processing is a growth area for Australia.

sources.title: Andrew Gee (Nats-Calare) - Maiden Speech

immigration.summary: On becoming Liberal Party leader in February 2026, Taylor named immigration his "top priority" and called for lower immigration and a policy that "puts the interests of Australians first," stating Australia should "deny access of immigrants from high risk regions who bring violence and hate."

sources.title: Angus Taylor

foreign_policy.summary: As Shadow Defence Minister, Taylor said on 15 July 2025 that Australia should raise its defence budget from around 2.3% of GDP to at least 3%, that this is vital to support commitments under the AUKUS pact, and that Australia must be prepared to act on Taiwan's security with clear, principled commitments from Canberra.

sources.title: Australia Must Be Ready to Support Taiwan, Says Angus Taylor

faith.summary: Anthony Albanese describes his Catholic faith as part of who he is, citing his Catholic upbringing as an influence on his values; he is generally described as a non-practising Catholic and was sworn in by affirmation rather than on a Bible.

sources.title: 'It's a part of who I am': Albanese on his Catholic faith

immigration.summary: As Prime Minister, Albanese's government in February 2023 abolished temporary protection visas, allowing up to 19,000 asylum seekers to stay in Australia permanently. In his 1996 maiden speech he expressed support for multiculturalism.

sources.title: Anthony Albanese

foreign_policy.summary: As Prime Minister, Albanese's government formally recognised the State of Palestine (announced August 2025, effective 21 September 2025) as part of a coordinated push for a two-state solution; he officially commenced the AUKUS security pact on 13 March 2023 (acquiring nuclear-powered submarines); restored high-level dialogue with China, meeting Xi Jinping in November 2022 and visiting China in November 2023; and pledged a further $100 million in aid to Ukraine in July 2022.

sources.title: Anthony Albanese

economic_nationalism.summary: Anthony Albanese has championed the "Future Made in Australia" agenda, stating "Our Government is investing in manufacturing to make more things here" and that government must "be more assertive in capitalising on our comparative advantages and building sovereign capability in areas of national interest." He cited "a new and widespread willingness to make economic interventions on the basis of national interest and national sovereignty."

sources.title: A future made in Australia

citizenship_eligibility.summary: Although birth to an Italian father would ordinarily confer Italian citizenship, his father was not recorded on his birth certificate, so he was not a dual citizen and met the eligibility requirements of section 44 of the Constitution.

sources.title: Anthony Albanese

sources.title: First Speech - Ash Ambihaipahar MP - Member for Barton NSW

faith.summary: Barnaby Joyce is a Roman Catholic.

sources.title: Wikipedia — Barnaby Joyce

immigration.summary: In September 2015 Joyce called for Australia to accept more Syrian refugees, with a preference to prioritise Christian refugees. In 2016 he opposed One Nation's proposed Muslim immigration ban, and he has stated his opposition to banning the burqa.

sources.title: Barnaby Joyce

foreign_policy.summary: On 10 August 2018, Joyce defended maintaining Australia's foreign aid budget, framing aid to Pacific island nations as a strategic necessity, saying Australia needs to support island nations 'otherwise the Chinese government will just move in there and support them.'

sources.title: Joyce defends foreign aid against farmers' criticism

economic_nationalism.summary: Barnaby Joyce has stated a preference for Australian ownership of agricultural assets, saying "My preference is always for the Australian people to be owner of the Australian asset" and "I'll never work to the exclusion of foreign investment but my preference is always for the Australian owner of the Australian asset." He supported blocking selected foreign bids (e.g. Kidman & Co) and warned against monopolistic foreign control of agricultural infrastructure.

sources.title: Barnaby Joyce's foreign investment odyssey

citizenship_eligibility.summary: Held New Zealand citizenship by descent from his father, which the High Court found rendered him ineligible under section 44(i); his 2016 election was declared invalid on 27 October 2017. Having renounced his New Zealand citizenship in August 2017, he was eligible to recontest and won the December 2017 New England by-election.

sources.title: Barnaby Joyce

immigration.summary: As Minister for Immigration and Citizenship (2010-2013), Bowen secured an offshore processing arrangement with Malaysia (the "Malaysia Solution") in 2011, under which Australia would transfer asylum seekers who arrived by boat to Malaysia in exchange for resettling refugees from there; the arrangement was struck down by the High Court in September 2011.

sources.title: Australia's asylum seeker policy history: a story of blunders and shame

foreign_policy.summary: In an ABC TV Insiders interview on 26 November 2023, Bowen said 'Israel can only really be secure and free when Palestine is secure and free, and ultimately, that is our long term objective,' reaffirmed that Israel has rights and obligations under international law, and said the government wanted ceasefire arrangements to become more permanent as the pathway to peace.

sources.title: Chris Bowen: Israel can only be free when Palestine is

economic_nationalism.summary: Chris Bowen has backed bringing solar panel manufacturing to Australia under the Future Made in Australia agenda, stating "We invented the modern solar panel... We've been missing, by and large, the middle part of manufacturing solar panels," and that the Hunter "will be centre of our Future Made in Australia," linking the policy to local jobs and strengthened supply chains.

sources.title: Sunshot program backs "Sun King" to bring large-scale solar panel manufacturing back to Australia

donors.summary: No donations disclosed directly to this member in AEC Member of Parliament returns (money received via a party is not attributed to individuals).

faith.summary: Dai Le is a lapsed Catholic who has stated she still believes in God and prays to the Virgin Mary, crediting her election win to the intercession of the Blessed Virgin Mary.

sources.title: Wikipedia — Dai Le

immigration.summary: Le, an independent and former refugee, takes a conditional view of migration, saying that if migration places are increased the government 'has the duty to ensure that there are immediate plans to build more housing, more public transport infrastructure and more local services,' and that government should 'first look locally.' She notes her electorate resettled nearly 10,000 refugees without additional infrastructure funding, and pledges to celebrate cultural diversity.

sources.title: Dai Le MP's First Speech in Parliament

economic_nationalism.summary: Dai Le has advocated for support of local small and medium manufacturing businesses, noting "Manufacturing is the largest employer in Fowler, standing at 40% yet the plan doesn't really invest in our local community, many of whom are small to medium sized manufacturing companies." She criticised the Future Made in Australia plan as inadequately supporting local manufacturers facing rising utility and input costs.

sources.title: Budget Reflections: A Missed Opportunity for Fowler and Australia

citizenship_eligibility.summary: After her 2022 election, questions were raised about her section 44 eligibility given her refugee background; she stated on her AEC declaration that she had never been a subject or citizen of any country other than Australia and confirmed the AEC accepted her nomination, rejecting suggestions of ineligibility.

sources.title: Dai Le rejects suggestions she's ineligible for Australia's parliament due to section 44

faith.summary: Husic was born to Bosnian Muslim immigrant parents and is the first Muslim elected to Australia's federal parliament; his household practised Islam while also celebrating Christmas and Easter, and after the September 11 attacks he described himself as a 'non-practising' Muslim. He took his ministerial oath on the Quran.

sources.title: Ed Husic

immigration.summary: In his maiden speech, Husic, the first Muslim member of federal parliament, praised migrants and multiculturalism, stating 'multiculturalism represents a vast reservoir of energy this nation can tap into,' and that harnessing talent 'from the first owners to the recently arrived' builds 'one of the greatest countries on the planet.'

sources.title: Ed Husic (ALP-Chifley) - Maiden Speech

foreign_policy.summary: Husic supports recognition of a Palestinian state and, after being dropped from cabinet, said on 12 May 2025 the government should do whatever it could to back Palestinian statehood as quickly as possible; he described mass starvation in Gaza as a conscious decision and the use of starvation as a war crime, and in May 2025 called for targeted sanctions against Israeli government ministers overseeing the military campaign in Gaza.

sources.title: Dumped cabinet minister Ed Husic urges Labor to push for Palestinian statehood
Ed Husic

economic_nationalism.summary: Ed Husic has advocated for rebuilding Australian domestic manufacturing and self-sufficiency, stating "Australia ranks last in a list of OECD countries in terms of manufacturing self-sufficiency" and "We want to send this signal to industry and its workers: Australian Labor is on the side of Australian manufacturing," describing manufacturing as "in the DNA of the Australian Labor Party."

sources.title: Manufacturing (speech)

immigration.summary: As Education Minister, Clare announced a cap of 270,000 international student enrolments for 2025 to create 'a better and a fairer system' by returning enrolments toward pre-pandemic levels, framing it as a response to housing affordability concerns linked to record migration and a crackdown on 'shonks' exploiting the sector.

faith.summary: Leeser is Jewish; he is described as the first Jewish Liberal member of the House of Representatives from New South Wales and a member of the Emanuel Synagogue in Woollahra. He announced he would wear a kippah in federal parliament for one week as a statement against rising antisemitism in Australia.

sources.title: Julian Leeser

foreign_policy.summary: Leeser supports Australian recognition of West Jerusalem as Israel's capital and called the Albanese government's 2022 reversal 'shambolic'. He opposes an immediate Gaza ceasefire while hostages are held and Hamas remains, saying a ceasefire would let terrorist organisations 'regroup and reform', and opposes imposing a timetable on a two-state solution 'without full and proper negotiations on the final-status issues'. After the October 2023 Hamas attack he called on Australia to suspend diplomatic relations with Iran.

sources.title: Julian Leeser
Speech to the House of Representatives - First Anniversary of the Hamas Terror Attacks on Israel

faith.summary: McCormack is a Roman Catholic; Wikipedia notes he, 'a Roman Catholic, married Catherine (nee Shaw) in Saint Michael's Cathedral, Wagga Wagga in 1986,' and he publicly identified himself 'as a proud Catholic' when selected to represent Australia at Pope Francis' 2025 funeral.

sources.title: Michael McCormack (Australian politician)
'Incredible honour': Pope Francis' funeral to have Riverina representation

immigration.summary: In a 2018 interview on regional migration, McCormack said "all options are on the table" for improving migration policy and supported "encouraging strategic targeted migration" to fill regional skill shortages, stating "Migrants bring a willingness to work. They start small businesses and their contribution is highly valued" and that regional migration "needs to be at the forefront of our immigration system."

sources.title: Riverina MP Michael McCormack: "all options are on the table" in response to claimed five-year regional migration visas

immigration.summary: In a 2003 workshop talk on multiculturalism, Plibersek argued that "the first and biggest lie is the one that suggests asylum seekers are a threat to this nation," noting Australia accepts a small number of refugees by world standards. She criticised Australia for choosing "the most expensive system for dealing with refugees - locking them up - or even more expensive, putting them on Nauru or Manus Island," and is a vocal advocate for multiculturalism.

foreign_policy.summary: In 2002 Plibersek described Israel as 'a rogue state' and Ariel Sharon as a 'war criminal', though she later developed friendly relations with the Jewish community and visited Israel and Palestine in 2014. As Shadow Foreign Minister in 2016 she advocated redrawing Australia's maritime border with East Timor. She opposed the 2003 invasion of Iraq and presented a letter signed by 43 Labor MPs opposing invasion without UN approval, and has opposed foreign aid cuts.

sources.title: Tanya Plibersek

immigration.summary: Wilkie considers Australia's bipartisan policy of mandatory detention 'immoral and illegal under numerous international agreements.' His Ending Indefinite and Arbitrary Immigration Detention Bill 2022 would effectively abolish offshore detention and move asylum seekers and refugees into the community with access to housing, support and the right to work. He cites detention costs of about $360,000 per person per year versus about $4,429 for someone living in the community, and condemns Australia's offshore processing on Nauru and Manus.

sources.title: Immoral and unlawful treatment of asylum seekers must end

foreign_policy.summary: Wilkie advocates a more independent Australian foreign policy underpinned by adherence to international law, supports increased overseas development assistance, and calls for Russian withdrawal from Ukraine. On Israel/Palestine he states Israel has been engaged in a 'decades-long, systematic campaign of occupation and oppression in Palestine' and that 'peace can only come with formal recognition of Palestine by all parties and a two-state solution,' calling for an end to Israel's campaign in Gaza and the West Bank. He argues Australia would be better served by a more independent security posture 'not beholden to' partnerships like ANZUS, the Quad and AUKUS.

sources.title: Where I stand

economic_nationalism.summary: On his "Where I stand" page, Wilkie states "Foreign investment needs much better regulation. Yes, Australia needs it, but only on our terms and only when it's genuinely in the national interest." He adds that "Assessment criteria must always include a national security test, and strategic assets including energy, transport infrastructure and broadacre prime agricultural land must never be sold or leased."

sources.title: Where I stand

From 2022 he held shadow responsibility for community safety, migrant services and multicultural affairs.

faith.summary: Josh Burns is Jewish. His maternal grandmother came to Australia as a Jewish refugee from Nazi Germany, he was educated at Mt Scopus Memorial College (a Jewish day school) and was active in the Jewish Labour Bund youth movement; he has spoken publicly as a Jewish person and is categorised among Jewish members of the Australian parliament.

sources.title: Josh Burns (politician)

immigration.summary: Burns has advocated for refugees and asylum seekers, moving a parliamentary motion urging the government to free refugees held in onshore and offshore detention (referenced June 2021). His advocacy is shaped by his family's history as migrants who fled persecution in Europe.

sources.title: Josh Burns (politician)

foreign_policy.summary: Burns, a Jewish Labor MP, supports a two-state solution and says he "desperately want[s] to see a peace agreement signed between the Israelis and the Palestinians." Following the 7 October 2023 attack he opposed an immediate ceasefire, arguing it would allow Hamas to regroup, and he opposed the government's vote in favour of UN General Assembly Resolution ES-10/22 on Gaza. In October 2022 he criticised the Albanese government for revoking Australia's recognition of Jerusalem as Israel's capital.

sources.title: Josh Burns (politician)

immigration.summary: Hill has championed Australian multiculturalism and 'the importance of Australia embracing its human diversity.' He made 'no apology' for championing reforms to the regulation of international education that were likely to reduce the number of international students granted visas, drawing criticism from the international education industry.

sources.title: Julian Hill (politician)

foreign_policy.summary: In a July 2024 statement, Hill said "Israel's Knesset vote against a Palestinian State is profoundly disappointing" and that "a two-state solution is the only realistic resolution to decades of violence," describing alternatives (one state) as "fantasies" or (apartheid or genocide) as "horrific." He called the acceleration of illegal West Bank settlements "appalling" and said major countries need to "collectively intervene... to impose consequences on Israel" to stop them.

sources.title: Julian Hill MP — Facebook statement on Knesset vote against a Palestinian State

economic_nationalism.summary: In a 2023 statement titled "Australia must make more things here," Hill said "We can make more things here. We should make more things here and we must make more things here," warning Australia is "around the lowest for manufacturing self-sufficiency of any developed country in the world" and "too vulnerable to supply shocks" and "to military blockades." He called for urgently boosting "sovereign manufacturing capability" while acknowledging "we can't make everything here."

sources.title: Australia must make more things here

immigration.summary: In her maiden speech (July 2019), Thwaites supported a 'humane approach to asylum seekers,' adding 'I believe this can be achieved while maintaining our sovereignty and our borders.' She has criticised the holding of asylum seekers in hotels as 'unsafe and cruel conditions,' said 'unnecessary cruelty is a hallmark of this government when it comes to treatment of asylum seekers,' and criticised refusal to take up New Zealand's resettlement offers.

sources.title: Kate Thwaites (ALP-Jagajaga) - Maiden Speech
Asylum Seekers

foreign_policy.summary: In a February 2024 parliamentary speech, Thwaites said she shared "the horror... at the destruction and death that have been occurring in Gaza under Israel's military action" and expressed devastation at Israel's planned Rafah ground offensive. She noted "Australia has voted for an immediate humanitarian ceasefire," said any ceasefire "cannot be a one-sided ceasefire" and "will also require Hamas to cease its attacks and to release the Israeli hostages," and said Australia expects Israel to act in accordance with the ICJ ruling.

sources.title: Gaza - Kate Thwaites MP

faith.summary: Mark Dreyfus is Jewish and has spoken of his personal Jewish faith, stating 'the central themes of law and justice in both Jewish religious and secular traditions have great meaning for me and have helped to shape my worldview' and referring to himself observing the holiday of Chanukah. Three of his great-grandparents perished in the Holocaust.

sources.title: Mark Dreyfus
Parliamentary Interfaith Breakfast - Mark Dreyfus KC MP

immigration.summary: As Attorney-General, Dreyfus promoted the Albanese Government's reforms to the refugee protection system (October 2023), stating Australians are 'rightly proud of our country's generous refugee program' and that reforms would give 'a fair go to genuine asylum seekers' while breaking 'the business model of people who seek to exploit the system' and not allowing 'people traffickers and other bad actors to exploit vulnerable migrants.'

sources.title: Restoring Integrity to our Protection System

foreign_policy.summary: In a parliamentary motion condemning Hamas, Dreyfus said "Australia unambiguously supports the right of Israel to defend itself" and that "the Australian Government stands as one with Israel and the people of Israel against Hamas," while also stating the government "will continue to support the legitimate rights and aspirations of the Palestinian people" and endorsing "a just and enduring two state solution – in which Israel and a future Palestinian state coexist in peace and security." He described Hamas as having "no interest in making peace with Israel."

sources.title: Motion condemning Hamas attacks on Israel - Mark Dreyfus KC MP

faith.summary: Peter Khalil is a Coptic Christian, born in Melbourne to Egyptian Coptic parents, and became the first Coptic Christian elected to the Australian Federal Parliament.

sources.title: Peter Khalil

immigration.summary: Khalil advocates a welcoming posture toward immigrants, crediting his parents' 1970 migration from Egypt, and stated migrants commit to Australia because of 'our values of freedom, of equality of opportunity, of a fair go.' During the COVID-19 pandemic (2020) he called for releasing detained refugees and asylum seekers who had cleared security checks, arguing it would protect their health and help slow the spread of the virus.

sources.title: Peter Khalil

foreign_policy.summary: Per his Wikipedia article, Khalil "is a strong supporter of the Western alliance" and a "very strong supporter of the US alliance," and on China co-authored a statement that "Bowing to Beijing would be the modern equivalent of the Munich Agreement," advocating "containment of Chinese totalitarian threats to democratic nations." He serves as Assistant Minister for Defence.

sources.title: Peter Khalil

immigration.summary: Marles supports turning back asylum-seeker boats and a Pacific Solution-style offshore resettlement of refugees, and as Shadow Minister for Immigration and Border Protection (2013-2016) backed maintaining offshore processing to deter people smuggling. He also supported the Australian War Memorial commemorating Operation Sovereign Borders navy personnel.

sources.title: Richard Marles

foreign_policy.summary: As Defence Minister, Marles is a strong supporter of AUKUS, saying it "is going to really drive the biggest uplift in the Australian defense industry that we've seen" and that the partnership requires contributing to the US submarine industrial base. On China he describes recent incidents as "unsafe and unprofessional" but seeks stabilisation, saying the nations should "cooperate where we can, disagree where we must" and advocates resuming defence dialogue to reduce miscalculation.

sources.title: EXCLUSIVE: Australian defense minister Richard Marles on AUKUS, China and industry concerns

economic_nationalism.summary: Outlining ALP defence policy as Shadow Defence Minister, Marles said "The ability to sustain and maintain the equipment the ADF uses is fundamental to our national security and it is essential that we always retain the industrial capability to meet this objective," that "At its best, Australian industry is as capable as any in the world," and advocated that "an Australian shipbuilder be mandated for all future shipbuilding programs."

sources.title: Shadow Defence Minister Richard Marles outlines ALP Defence policy

sources.title: Tim Wilson (Australian politician)

foreign_policy.summary: Wilson is a member of the 'Wolverines', a parliamentary group critical of the Chinese government, and has accused Huawei of creating surveillance systems for the Chinese government, urging the UK to ban the company. In an October 2024 advertisement in the Australian Jewish News he described himself as 'proudly Zionist'.

sources.title: Tim Wilson (Australian politician)

economic_nationalism.summary: In his 2016 maiden speech, Tim Wilson championed open markets and free trade, saying his predecessor's legacy was "to open markets" and that "the benefits of trade agreements begin with the signing of a pen but finish with new opportunity." He framed Australia as a net capital importer needing a competitive tax system to attract foreign investment, and advocated restructuring domestic sectors into "outward-looking export industries" rather than protecting local industry through tariffs.

faith.summary: Hastie is a Christian; after rejecting religion in adolescence he later became part of several reformed and evangelical churches (including Capitol Hill Baptist Church, St Matthew's Anglican Church Shenton Park, Crossroads Church Canberra, and Mandurah Presbyterian Church), and has cited his Christian faith as informing his policy views such as on individual freedoms.

sources.title: Andrew Hastie - Wikipedia

immigration.summary: Andrew Hastie has stated that Australia's immigration numbers are 'too high, while the standards are too low,' arguing population growth has outpaced infrastructure and pressured housing and services and that 'the system needs a massive overhaul' (March 2026). In October 2025 he resigned from the Liberal shadow frontbench, saying he could not remain Shadow Home Affairs Minister 'and remain silent on immigration policy.'

sources.title: Andrew Hastie rallies the right on immigration levels at conservative conference in Perth
Andrew Hastie quits Liberal front bench over immigration row
Andrew Hastie

foreign_policy.summary: Hastie is a prominent critic of Chinese government influence in Australia, having written that 'Communist China' rather than Islamic terrorism would be the central security concern of the 21st century, and supported banning Huawei from Australia's 5G network. In May 2020 he signed a letter condemning what it called an assault on Hong Kong's autonomy, rule of law and fundamental freedoms.

sources.title: Andrew Hastie

economic_nationalism.summary: Andrew Hastie, Shadow Minister for Industry and Sovereign Capability, has argued for rebuilding Australian manufacturing as a matter of sovereignty, stating "Our need for a reborn Australian manufacturing sector is now vital. Our sovereignty depends upon it, our allies and partners depend upon it." He defined self-reliance as "the ability of Australia to domestically manufacture the goods and services we need," said "if globalisation led to the offshoring of Australian manufacturing, then the new geopolitical disorder can bring it back home," and called for governments to support and "pick winners" in industry.

sources.title: Hastie speech signals Opposition emphasis on national resilience

In 2016 she spoke against then-Immigration Minister Peter Dutton's comments regarding Lebanese Muslim immigration.

Anne Aly

foreign_policy.summary: On recognition of Palestinian statehood, Aly stated that 'just saying to recognize Palestinian state without any context is in some ways tokenistic', supporting the government position that recognition should be part of a just peace process. She is also noted as a researcher on countering violent extremism, having founded People Against Violent Extremism (PaVE).

economic_nationalism.summary: In her first speech, Zaneta Mascarenhas argued "in WA we need to build beyond the resources boom and make our economy more resilient," and that "Australia needs to increase diversification, commercialisation and sophistication of our industry capability." She said Australia is rich in critical minerals to "build batteries, wind turbines and solar panels" and could become a "renewable energy superpower," advocating use of the National Reconstruction Fund to build advanced manufacturing capability, including the cell-manufacturing "value-add step."

sources.title: Proud moment for Indian-Australians: First Speech - Ms Zaneta Mascarenhas MP

faith.summary: David Pocock has publicly spoken about the role of his Christian faith in inspiring him to get involved in politics, citing theologians such as Dietrich Bonhoeffer, Oscar Romero and Walter Wink as influences. He has described exploring his Christian faith, including reading theology and studying writers such as N.T. Wright and John Howard Yoder.

sources.title: David Pocock elected to Senate

immigration.summary: Pocock opposes offshore processing, calling for an end to it and a more human-rights-compliant refugee approach. After the government reauthorised Nauru offshore detention in February 2023 he called it a 'massive failure' and argued it 'makes no sense to be paying $420m of taxpayer money over three years to detain a small group of people.'

sources.title: David Pocock blasts Albanese government as it reauthorises Nauru offshore immigration detention
Immigration & Refugees

foreign_policy.summary: In remarks reported on 3 June 2024, Pocock called on Australia to cut all military ties with Israel and end arms deals, citing Australian-made parts used in F-35 aircraft and an Elbit Systems vehicle contract, and called for targeted sanctions against members of the Israeli government and Israel Defense Forces over the situation in Gaza.

sources.title: Lawmaker urges Australian gov't to end arms deals with Israel

economic_nationalism.summary: Independent Senator David Pocock has advocated reforming government procurement to favour Australian companies and small businesses, saying Australia should be "investing in Australian companies - sovereign Australian companies - that are building up their capability in a whole range of industries from defence to quantum computing... and not just going with multinationals." He criticised multinationals "skimming the cream off the top and subcontracting that to smaller Australian companies" as "a ridiculous use of taxpayer money," and called for a system where smaller Australian companies can win contracts.

sources.title: Small businesses deserve a fair shot at winning government procurement contracts says Senator Pocock

citizenship_eligibility.summary: Pocock, who held Zimbabwean and South African citizenship by birth/descent, provided documentary evidence renouncing both before the nomination deadline of 22 April 2022, leaving him solely an Australian citizen as required by section 44 ahead of his 2022 Senate election.

foreign_policy.summary: As Foreign Minister, Wong said in April 2024 that Australia would consider recognising Palestinian statehood as a means of facilitating a two-state solution, but clarified the May 2024 UN vote on Palestinian participation did not constitute recognition, which would come only "when we think the time is right." She described Israel's planned Rafah offensive as "unjustifiable" (February 2024), paused UNRWA funding in February 2024 pending Israeli evidence, and in June 2025 announced Australia would sanction Israeli ministers Ben-Gvir and Smotrich. She sought to stabilise relations with China, becoming the first Australian cabinet minister to visit China since 2019 (December 2022).

sources.title: Penny Wong

faith.summary: In 2014 Lambie described herself as 'Catholic; I'm religious', citing it as a reason for declining an invitation to visit a Sydney mosque.

sources.title: Jacqui Lambie

immigration.summary: Lambie's stated immigration positions have shifted over time. In 2014-2017 she proposed banning the burqa, introduced a bill to prohibit full face coverings during terrorism threat declarations, called for Australia to adopt travel restrictions on Muslim-majority countries similar to Donald Trump's, and advocated deporting Muslims who supported Sharia law. After participating in the 2018 program 'Go Back to Where You Came From,' she shifted toward a more pro-refugee stance, saying the discussion about accepting more refugees "needs to be on the political table," and distanced herself from earlier anti-Islam rhetoric.

sources.title: Jacqui Lambie

foreign_policy.summary: Lambie has been a vocal critic of the Chinese government, warning in 2014 of the "unprecedented" threat from "Communist Chinese military capacity" and opposing the China-Australia Free Trade Agreement in 2015, calling the Chinese government "anti-democratic" and "a bully, thief, liar and international human rights abuser." On AUKUS, she criticised the revised submarine arrangement, telling defence officials in Senate estimates "What we should be getting is three new submarines and now we're getting trash instead."

sources.title: Jacqui Lambie
Capability that matters: submarine switch played down

economic_nationalism.summary: Through her "Make Australia Make Again" (MAMA) campaign, Lambie calls for reviving domestic manufacturing, arguing "What we make, we control" and that government "has an obligation to identify the core industrial capabilities Australia needs." She cites the Whyalla steelworks as a "critical" sovereign capability, advocates putting "the brakes on foreign purchases of our critical assets," and promotes a "Back local. Back Australia" approach of investing in Australian industry rather than competitors.

sources.title: MAMA Make Australia Make Again

citizenship_eligibility.summary: Lambie held British citizenship by descent from her Scottish-born father and resigned from the Senate on 14 November 2017 under section 44 over this dual citizenship; following a recount her seat went to Steve Martin, and she was re-elected to the Senate in 2019.

sources.title: Jacqui Lambie

abortion.summary: Barr voted Aye in the ACT Legislative Assembly division of 25 June 2024 to agree to the Health (Improved Abortion Access) Amendment Bill 2024, which allowed nurse practitioners and authorised midwives to prescribe abortion medication and required conscientious objectors to provide referral information.

sources.title: Debates Weekly Hansard, Tenth Assembly, 25 June 2024

voluntary_assisted_dying.summary: Voted in favour of the Voluntary Assisted Dying Bill 2023, which passed the ACT Legislative Assembly 20 votes to 5 on 5 June 2024 (recorded in the Ayes).

sources.title: Debates Weekly Hansard, Tenth Assembly, 5 June 2024

gender_lgbtq.summary: Stated position and record: Barr is Australia's first openly gay head of a state or territory government, was a prominent advocate for the 'Yes' campaign in the 2017 same-sex marriage postal survey, and his government passed the 2020 legislation banning LGBTI conversion practices.

sources.title: Andrew Barr

religious_freedom.summary: In the ACT Legislative Assembly debate on the Discrimination Amendment Bill 2022 (23 March 2023), Barr spoke in support of the bill, which narrowed exceptions for religious bodies so that, when providing public goods, services or employment, they may discriminate only on the ground of a person's religious conviction and not on other protected attributes such as sexuality, gender identity or marital status, while leaving greater freedom for religious observances such as the training and ordination of clergy. This is a stated position and a recorded contribution to debate.

sources.title: Week 2 - Thursday, 23 March 2023 - 10th Assembly Hansard

drugs_law_order.summary: Barr supports drug law liberalisation as harm reduction: as Chief Minister he introduced a 2018 bill to legalise personal cannabis cultivation and possession, and in 2021 introduced amendments to decriminalise possession of small quantities of illicit drugs, stating the 'just say no' approach has not stopped drug use and that the solution lies in health-based harm reduction. (Stated position.)

sources.title: Journey to drug decriminalisation

(Stated position; moved repeal motion.)

drugs_law_order.summary: Labor Minister for Police and Counter-terrorism. She defended the Government's 2024 youth bail laws on radio, saying "repeated bail without backup, with no intervention whatsoever, is just a road to ruin." She backed permanent police "wanding" powers to detect knives (commenced December 2024), saying "knife crime devastates families and communities." In the Government's October 2025 response to the 2024 Drug Summit she said police would "remain focused on disrupting the supply of illicit drugs" while ensuring "diversion opportunities are available."

religious_freedom.summary: As Attorney General, Daley introduced and championed the Conversion Practices Ban Bill 2024 (passed 22 March 2024). He said conversion practices 'cause significant ongoing trauma' and that the law was developed 'following extensive consultation to ensure that it strikes the right balance between protecting vulnerable communities as well as legitimate religious and cultural practices,' adding the government did 'not intend to ban the teachings of a religious leader or expression of a religious belief through sermon.' He later reaffirmed in parliament that 'praying with or over a particular person in an attempt to try to change or suppress that person's sexuality or gender is unlawful.'

sources.title: NSW Government introduces laws to ban harmful LGBTQ+ conversion practices
Prayer ban reaffirmed in New South Wales, Australia

drugs_law_order.summary: Labor Attorney-General. He announced the 2024 youth bail law changes and the new "post and boast" (performance crime) offence (passed 22 March 2024), saying the bail laws were "purposefully designed to address repeated alleged offending by young people aged between 14 and 18" and that posting footage advertising crimes "is unacceptable and has to stop." He supported the expansion of pre-court drug diversion (Early Drug Diversion Initiative), calling it "key to alleviating pressure on our court system," and led the Government's October 2025 response to the 2024 Drug Summit which supported over 50 of 56 recommendations including a pill-testing trial and reformed drug diversion.

sources.title: New bail and performance crime laws passed to prevent youth crime
NSW Government delivers response to the 2024 Drug Summit Report

gender_lgbtq.summary: Independent MP for Sydney and an openly gay member of the NSW Legislative Assembly (married to Victor Hoeld). A leading LGBTIQ law-reform advocate: former co-chair of Australian Marriage Equality and a key leader of the 2017 'Yes' marriage equality campaign. He introduced and championed the Equality Legislation Amendment (LGBTIQA+) Bill 2023, which passed the Legislative Assembly 50 votes to 30 (16 Oct 2024) and the Legislative Council 15-12 (17 Oct 2024) in amended form, removing barriers to gender recognition (allowing trans people to change birth certificates without surgery). He described the decision to strip back parts of the bill as 'heartbreaking.' In Sept 2024 the Federal Court found a tweet by Mark Latham about Greenwich was defamatory and homophobic, awarding him $140,000.

sources.title: Out Gay Sydney MP Alex Greenwich Reflects On A Decade In NSW Parliament
NSW Passes Equality Bill, Granting Landmark Changes For The Trans Community
Reduced NSW equality bill passes, discrimination remains

religious_freedom.summary: Greenwich (Independent MP) dissented from the parliamentary committee report endorsing Mark Latham's religious-freedom bill and, with Jenny Leong, urged the Attorney-General to disregard it, warning against enshrining protections for discrimination 'under the guise of religious freedoms.' He championed the Conversion Practices Ban Bill 2024, saying on its passage that 'NSW wakes up a safer place for LGBTQIA+ people.'

sources.title: NSW Parliament Panel Endorses Mark Latham's 'Discriminatory' Religious Freedom Laws
Conversion therapy ban passes NSW parliament after marathon overnight debate

drugs_law_order.summary: Independent MP for Sydney who advocates drug law reform. He supports decriminalisation as a harm-reduction approach (replacing convictions with cautions, fines and health referrals), pill testing, and removing the possibility of custodial sentences for adults possessing small quantities of cannabis. He has disclosed holding a medicinal cannabis prescription, saying "Like over 100,000 people in NSW, I have a script for medical cannabis. It helps me manage anxiety and insomnia," and has campaigned for a medical defence so prescribed cannabis users are not penalised for driving when unimpaired, noting "THC can be detected for days, if not weeks, after it no longer impacts driving."

sources.title: Alex Greenwich Fights For Reform & Shares He Has A Medicinal Cannabis Prescription Himself

religious_freedom.summary: Premier Minns' government introduced the Conversion Practices Ban Bill 2024 (announced 13 March 2024, passed 22 March 2024). The government said it developed the legislation 'following extensive consultation' to balance protecting vulnerable communities with 'legitimate religious and cultural practices,' and stated it did not intend to ban religious teaching or expression of belief through a sermon.

sources.title: NSW Government introduces laws to ban harmful LGBTQ+ conversion practices

drugs_law_order.summary: Labor Premier. As Opposition Leader before the March 2023 election he supported a drug summit, pill testing, decriminalisation of personal drug use and cannabis law reform, but as Premier announced at the December 2024 NSW Drug Summit that decriminalisation was "off the table," arguing he had no mandate. He championed police knife "wanding" powers (modelled on Queensland's Jack's Law), saying at their December 2024 commencement that the laws would be "inconvenient" but "we can't take chances when it comes to public safety." He defended and extended the youth bail laws in February 2025, stating "the bail laws are working, so we're extending them."

sources.title: Police wanding powers to tackle knife crime begin this week
The number of kids refused bail in NSW has spiked. The premier sees it as a win, but critics say it's a fail

drugs_law_order.summary: Labor Minister for Health. Following the December 2024 NSW Drug Summit he announced a 12-month pill/drug checking trial at music festivals starting early 2025, saying "if there are any further steps we can take to keep young people safe this festival season we'll listen" and that the Government "will be guided by that which delivers in the best interests of the health of the community." In the October 2025 Drug Summit response he described drug reform as traversing "health, law and order, education, housing and so much more."

gender_lgbtq.summary: Liberal MP for North Shore. She crossed the floor to support the Equality Legislation Amendment (LGBTIQA+) Bill 2024, defying Liberal party lines (the party did not grant a conscience vote). She said: 'As a Liberal I believe in the fundamental principle of equality for all people, regardless of their gender identity or sexual orientation,' and 'Just because the party won't give you a conscience vote, doesn't mean that you don't have a conscience.' She argued the bill 'will not harm any person in NSW' and called claims it would create risk for women 'fearmongering.' She had previously supported the 'Yes' vote for marriage equality.

sources.title: Felicity Wilson defies party

drugs_law_order.summary: Legalise Cannabis Party MLC and the party's first NSW member, elected 2023. A leading cannabis-reform advocate, he introduced the Drug Misuse and Trafficking Amendment (Regulation of Personal Adult Use of Cannabis) Bill 2023 in the Legislative Council on 29 November 2023, which would let adults possess small quantities of cannabis, cultivate up to six plants and gift cannabis to other adults (debate adjourned at second reading). He chaired Portfolio Committee No. 1's inquiry into the cannabis regulatory framework (appointed 20 March 2024); its final report (20 June 2025) called existing cannabis laws "unjust and ineffective" and recommended abolishing custodial sentences for minor possession and a staged move toward decriminalisation and legal regulated access.

gender_lgbtq.summary: Labor MLC (since 2005), a Catholic who has been a consistent opponent of LGBTQ-affirming legislation. He opposed a NSW Greens marriage equality bill (2012); in May 2017 was one of three Labor MPs to vote with the Coalition to block decriminalisation of abortion; wrote against same-sex adoption in NSW; and wrote an opinion piece opposing the Safe Schools program (Nov 2016). He publicly urged people to sign a petition calling on the Legislative Council to vote against Alex Greenwich's Equality Bill, arguing the reforms risked 'far-reaching unintended consequences for women and children' and would let 'males legally identify as females' and access women-only spaces. In June 2023 he spoke at a 'Why Can't Women Talk About Sex' event alongside Moira Deeming and Tania Mihailuk.

drugs_law_order.summary: Greens MLC and justice spokesperson who has consistently opposed the Minns Government's youth bail law changes (section 22C of the Bail Act 2013). On the February 2025 three-year extension of the laws she said sending more young people to prison was "an indictment on the premier," criticising the government for gauging success on prison numbers. In an August 2025 statement she described the laws as "locking up more children and young people, for longer, before they have even been found guilty," noted nearly 60% of detained youth are First Nations, said "locking up kids is the most criminogenic thing we can do to them," and called instead to "invest in diversion, prevention, and community-led programs, not more prison beds for kids."

sources.title: Record surge in youth detention exposes Minns Government's failed bail laws
The number of kids refused bail in NSW has spiked. The premier sees it as a win, but critics say it's a fail

gender_lgbtq.summary: One Nation MLC (sat as independent for part of the term) known for vocal opposition to transgender and LGBTIQ measures. He introduced the Education Legislation Amendment (Parental Rights) Bill, which sought to prohibit teaching of 'gender fluidity' in schools, restrict trans students' use of facilities and sports, and require school staff to disclose a student's gender identity to parents; the NSW Government rejected it. During the Conversion Practices Ban Bill 2024 debate he opposed the late-night sitting, calling it a 'shameful tactic' and the 'politics of exhaustion,' and moved a motion to delay the debate. In Sept 2024 the Federal Court ruled a tweet he posted about Alex Greenwich was defamatory and homophobic.

sources.title: NSW Government Rejects One Nation MLC Mark Latham's Anti-Trans Education Bill
Alex Greenwich
Conversion therapy bill passes NSW parliament after marathon debate

religious_freedom.summary: Latham (One Nation MLC) has been a leading parliamentary advocate for religious-freedom and parental-rights legislation in NSW. He introduced the Anti-Discrimination Amendment (Religious Freedoms and Equality) Bill, which a NSW parliamentary committee chaired by Gabrielle Upton said had 'merit and could be used as a template for reform'; a version of the religious-freedom bill was defeated in the Legislative Council 29-4 in February 2022, with government and opposition uniting against it. He has also promoted an Education Legislation Amendment (Parental Rights) Bill to restrict teaching about gender, and in March 2023 gave a church speech on 'religious freedom, parental rights, school education.' During the March 2024 conversion-practices debate he opposed the overnight sitting, calling it a 'shameful tactic' and the 'politics of exhaustion.'

sources.title: Mark Latham
Mark Latham's Religious Freedom Bill Defeated In NSW Parliament
NSW Parliament Panel Endorses Mark Latham's 'Discriminatory' Religious Freedom Laws
Conversion therapy ban passes NSW parliament after marathon overnight debate

During the Conversion Practices Ban Bill 2024 debate he moved an amendment to remove 'gender identity' from the bill, prioritising individual autonomy in choosing counselling. He said: 'We support individuals or institutions offering a counselling service that attempts to convince people that they are not same-sex attracted or that they are not transgender,' while adding he did not support practices involving 'physical assault or mental anguish' as those are already crimes. The amendment was defeated.

abortion.summary: Stated position / recorded action: As Greens leader, Cassy O'Connor supported the 13 June 2018 motion to provide terminations through public hospitals (defeated 12-11). In a 3 February 2023 statement she said a Women's Health Tasmania report "highlights the need for improved access to safe and affordable termination services in Tasmania" and described "a health system still failing on reproductive health."

sources.title: Tasmanian Parliament Has Opposed A Move To Address The State's Abortion Access Issues
Reproductive Health Report Demands Action

voluntary_assisted_dying.summary: Recorded vote: as the Greens member for Clark in the House of Assembly, Cassy O'Connor (listed as 'Ms O'Connor') voted Aye in favour of the End-of-Life Choices (Voluntary Assisted Dying) Bill 2020 at its second reading division on 4 December 2020. The bill passed 17 Ayes to 7 Noes. (She now sits in the Legislative Council for Hobart.)

sources.title: House of Assembly Report of Debates, Friday 4 December 2020 (second reading division, End-of-Life Choices (Voluntary Assisted Dying) Bill 2020)

gender_lgbtq.summary: As Greens leader O'Connor championed the 2019 gender-optional birth certificate reforms, saying "Just as it was with the vote on marriage equality, the sky is likely to remain in place with the passing of this legislation"; in a February 2023 parliamentary speech she supported a ban on conversion practices, stating "The Greens choose love, acceptance and inclusion" and speaking as the mother of a transgender son. (Stated positions/parliamentary speech.)

sources.title: Tasmania passes landmark laws for gender-optional birth certificates
Legislation to Prohibit Conversion Practices

religious_freedom.summary: O'Connor has opposed weakening section 17 of the Anti-Discrimination Act, describing it as 'gold standard' and raising concern about the Australian Christian Lobby campaign to water it down (Sept 2021), and has supported legislation prohibiting conversion practices while distinguishing such practices from legitimate religious prayer and pastoral care (Feb 2023). Stated positions.

sources.title: Premier - Anti-Discrimination Act
Legislation to Prohibit Conversion Practices

drugs_law_order.summary: Stated position: O'Connor, Greens justice spokesperson, advocates treating drug use as a health issue rather than a crime and supports decriminalisation, saying in January 2023 that 'Drug use is a health issue, not a crime against another person' and that criminalisation is 'costly, ineffective, and harmful.' In October 2024 she called on the Rockliff government to 'look at the evidence from other jurisdictions, including the ACT, and introduce this common-sense reform in Tasmania.'

sources.title: CLC Report Should Prompt Informed Debate on Drug Law Reform
Drug Decriminalisation Success in ACT - Tassie Should Follow Lead

She was one of five members who voted no when the bill passed the Legislative Council 9 votes to 5 on 21 November 2013.

Stated position.

abortion.summary: Allan, member for Bendigo East since 1999, voted for the Abortion Law Reform Act 2008 that decriminalised abortion in Victoria; her Wikipedia biography notes she received abusive emails and a picket of her electorate office by anti-abortion campaigners over the vote and was targeted by Right to Life groups in the 2010 campaign. As Premier in October 2024 she said there are 'real and genuine threats to the protections women have fought for and won' and that legislation to protect those rights 'is being looked at being torn down at too many opportunities by conservative politicians.'

sources.title: Jacinta Allan
We must keep fighting for abortion rights, Victorian Premier Jacinta Allan says

gender_lgbtq.summary: Labor MP for Bendigo East and Premier of Victoria (since September 2023). She has been a vocal supporter of LGBTIQ+ equality, declaring in a February 2025 statement that 'in Victoria, equality is not negotiable' and pledging to defend LGBTIQ+ rights against 'hard-right extremists.' Her government cites achievements including the conversion-practices ban, same-sex adoption, easier birth-certificate sex changes for trans and gender-diverse Victorians, the Pride Centre, and landmark anti-vilification laws.

sources.title: Love Who You Want To Love – And Be Who You Are

drugs_law_order.summary: As Premier, Allan announced in April 2024 that the government would not proceed with a second medically supervised injecting room in Melbourne's CBD, saying a site balancing user support and community backing could not be found and committing instead to expanded health and addiction supports. In March 2025 she announced and her government passed the 'toughest bail laws in the country', stating 'I have listened to victims of crime and Victorians, and I have acted', removing remand as a last resort and making community safety the overarching principle. She also announced Australia's first machete ban (effective 1 September 2025), saying 'Machetes are destroying lives so we will destroy machetes.'

sources.title: Vic government drops CBD injecting room against advice

gender_lgbtq.summary: Liberal MP for Berwick; elected Victorian Liberal leader in December 2024 after John Pesutto's defamation loss to Moira Deeming, serving as Opposition Leader until November 2025. He had opposed Deeming's expulsion and, on becoming leader, promoted Deeming allies and later elevated Deeming to a shadow assistant ministry. In 2015 he voted against legislation allowing same-sex couples to adopt. By 2025 he said he supports Victoria's abortion laws and would be happy to attend Melbourne's pride march, and stated 'every person has a choice to identify or be who they want,' while also announcing a Coalition government would stop biological men convicted of sex offences being housed in women's prisons.

We must give young offenders every chance to turn their lives around.' He earlier served as Shadow Minister for Police (2021-2022).

gender_lgbtq.summary: Liberal MP for Hawthorn and Leader of the Opposition / Victorian Liberal leader from December 2022 until a leadership spill on 27 December 2024; factionally a moderate. He is central to the Moira Deeming affair: after Deeming spoke at the 'Let Women Speak' rally (18 March 2023) that was gatecrashed by neo-Nazis, Pesutto moved to discipline her, leading to her nine-month suspension and then expulsion from the party room (19-11) on 12 May 2023. Deeming sued him for defamation over imputations she associated with neo-Nazis; in December 2024 the Federal Court found Pesutto had defamed her, awarding $300,000 in damages plus large legal costs. As leader he stated that 'all people, irrespective of faith, sexual preference, race, ethnicity or gender they identify with, have an equal place in Victoria.'

sources.title: Moira Deeming

abortion.summary: Deeming (Liberal) holds anti-abortion views. Her Wikipedia biography states she 'believes that laws legalising abortion need to be repealed' and 'thinks that rape victims should reject abortions and turn to the church.' Writing on a Christian news website she described abortion as a 'terrible evil' and wrote that each abortion 'wounds a mother and father, murders an unborn child, and ends a whole family line.'

sources.title: Moira Deeming

gender_lgbtq.summary: Liberal MLC for Western Metropolitan and a prominent social conservative on gender issues. She co-organised and spoke at the 'Let Women Speak' (Kellie-Jay Keen-Minshull / 'Posie Parker') rally on the steps of Parliament House in Melbourne on 18 March 2023, which was gatecrashed by members of the neo-Nazi National Socialist Network who performed Nazi salutes. After the rally, then-Liberal leader John Pesutto moved to expel her; she accepted a nine-month suspension from the parliamentary Liberal Party on 27 March 2023, then was expelled from the party room by 19 votes to 11 on 12 May 2023. She sued Pesutto for defamation over imputations that she associated with neo-Nazis; in December 2024 the Federal Court found Pesutto had defamed her and awarded $300,000 in damages. She was readmitted to the parliamentary Liberal Party on 27 December 2024. Deeming has campaigned against transgender 'gender ideology', opposed allowing trans women in female sport, criticised the Safe Schools program, and has been a vocal advocate for what she frames as women's sex-based rights. In October 2023 she moved a motion in the Legislative Council calling for a parliamentary inquiry into gender-affirming care; it was defeated 21-16, with the Liberals, Nationals and conservative crossbenchers voting in support and Labor and the Greens opposing.

sources.title: Moira Deeming
Moira Deeming: Victorian MP wins $300,000 in defamation battle against State Liberal leader John Pesutto
Moira Deeming's Anti-Trans Motion Defeated Despite Liberal Votes

religious_freedom.summary: Deeming, a Presbyterian, is a prominent religious-conservative voice in the Legislative Council. Her Wikipedia biography records her conservative-Christian positions, including the view that rape victims should reject abortion and turn to the church, and documents her opposition to a range of progressive social measures. She supported the July 2024 petition to retain the Lord's Prayer in the Legislative Council, telling Parliament she had been 'overwhelmed by the number of religious communities - not necessarily Christian communities' who wanted the prayer retained.

sources.title: Moira Deeming

gender_lgbtq.summary: Liberal MLC for Eastern Victoria, elected 2022. A social conservative and lifelong member of the City Builders' church (led in Australia by her father, pastor Brian Heath), an organisation that has been accused of historic links to gay conversion practices. Before the 2022 election, then-Liberal leader Matthew Guy said Heath would not be permitted to sit in the parliamentary party room if elected, citing the church's opposition to gay, transgender and reproductive rights; new leader John Pesutto reversed that decision and appointed her Secretary of the Parliamentary Liberal Party. She was a close ally of Moira Deeming and was demoted as party-room Secretary on 12 May 2023 during the caucus vote on Deeming's expulsion. In October 2025 her sister Clare Heath-McIvor testified to a Victorian parliamentary inquiry about conversion practices allegedly linked to the church. In October 2023 she voted for Moira Deeming's motion calling for an inquiry into gender-affirming care (defeated 21-16).

sources.title: Renee Heath
Opposition Leader Says Ultra-Conservative Candidate Won't Sit In Party Room If Elected

religious_freedom.summary: Heath, a conservative Pentecostal and lifelong member of the City Builders' church (led by her father), framed religious liberty as a central theme of her inaugural speech (20 December 2022), stating: 'Victorians should not be told what to think, what to say, how to worship, how to raise their children,' which she characterised as 'a failed socialist experiment which has reinvented itself as a cultural movement.'

religious_freedom.summary: Mulholland (Liberal) sponsored the petition (nearly 11,000 signatures) to retain the Lord's Prayer in Legislative Council proceedings, debated 31 July 2024. He argued 'This place is deeply rooted in traditions and our Christian foundations that have made our democracy so strong,' and wrote that 'any attempt to remove faith from Parliament - the public square - must be resisted at all costs,' adding that representatives 'make decisions informed by our values, and those values at times are inseparable from our faith.' The Labor government subsequently abandoned plans to replace the prayer.

drugs_law_order.summary: Liberal MLC. Mulholland introduced the Bail Amendment (Indictable Offences Whilst on Bail) Bill 2024 (first reading 20 February 2024), a private member's bill to retain the offence of committing an indictable offence while on bail and prevent its repeal under the 2023 bail changes, automatically elevating the bail test for those who reoffend while on bail. The bill's second reading was defeated on 6 March 2024.

In October 2025 she co-authored, with Georgie Purcell, a report calling for medical and surgical abortion to be made free to every Victorian, expanded surgical provision in public hospitals, extending medical abortion access from nine to twelve weeks, and oversight of conscientious objection.

gender_lgbtq.summary: Legalise Cannabis MLC for South-Eastern Metropolitan (since 2022) and an LGBTIQ advocate. On Transgender Day of Remembrance (28 November 2024) she made a Legislative Council statement in solidarity with trans and gender-diverse people, citing high rates of violence and suicidal thoughts and criticising parliamentarians who use 'child safety' as a pretext for vilification. She has supported gender-affirming care for minors, anti-vilification laws and LGBTIQ+ legal services, and praised Minister Harriet Shing's 'unwavering allyship.'

drugs_law_order.summary: Legalise Cannabis Party MLC. With David Ettershank, Payne co-introduced the Road Safety Amendment (Medicinal Cannabis) Bill 2023 to end automatic licence loss for unimpaired medicinal cannabis drivers; the resulting reform took effect 1 March 2025. She voted in support of the government's Pill Testing Bill 2024 (passed 29 October 2024) and introduced the Drugs, Poisons and Controlled Substances Amendment (Regulation of Personal Adult Use of Cannabis) Bill 2023 to allow personal adult cannabis use.

He has spoken about his rights being 'on public trial' and said the parliament 'needs more queer voices... more trans and gender-diverse voices.' He advocates decriminalisation of personal drug use, 'directing people who use drugs to health services for help, not prison for harm,' plus banning sniffer dogs at festivals and increased harm-reduction funding.

gender_lgbtq.summary: Labor MLC for Eastern Victoria (since 2014) and the first openly lesbian member of the Parliament of Victoria. In June 2022 she was appointed Minister for Equality (alongside Steve Dimopoulos, becoming one of Victoria's first openly gay cabinet ministers). She was a vocal supporter of the Change or Suppression (Conversion) Practices Prohibition Bill, speaking in the February 2021 debate to acknowledge survivors and the harms of conversion practices, and has publicly criticised the Liberal Party's record on LGBTQI protections. She is a member of the Labor Left. As Minister for Equality she spoke against Moira Deeming's October 2023 motion for an inquiry into gender-affirming care (defeated 21-16). From April 2026 she became Minister for Health, Ambulance Services and Water.

sources.title: Harriet Shing

gender_lgbtq.summary: Labor MLA (Armadale) and Attorney General. Buti is leading the Cook Labor Government's commitment to ban LGBTIQA+ conversion practices. He stated: "Conversion practices that are used to attempt to suppress or change an individual's sexual orientation or gender identity are not therapeutic and they cause mental health harm to LGBTIQA+ people" and "The Cook Labor Government is committed to banning LGBTIQA+ conversion practices." He said legislation to criminalise conversion therapy, with a complementary civil response scheme for survivors, would be introduced to parliament in 2026, with continued stakeholder engagement on drafting.

sources.title: Tony Buti commits to introducing conversion therapy ban in 2026

religious_freedom.summary: As WA Attorney General following the March 2025 election, Buti announced that a ban on conversion practices would be introduced to Parliament by the end of 2025, with a model including 'criminal prohibition and a civil response scheme.' Critics noted the proposed religious protection covers only 'a mere expression of belief or religious principle which is not intended to change or suppress an individual's sexual orientation or gender identity.' The Cook Government, in which Buti serves, has committed to reforming and narrowing the religious exemptions under the Equal Opportunity Act and to strengthening protections for LGBTIQA+ staff and students in religious schools. His office's response to Groenewald's June 2026 question on Christian freedom of expression committed to legislation that 'achieves a balance between the rights and interests of the diverse community.'

drugs_law_order.summary: Whitby is Minister for Police; Road Safety; Tourism; Great Southern in the Cook Labor Government, overseeing the Western Australia Police Force. His portfolio includes enforcement of WA's knife and edged-weapon laws, which took effect on 20 December 2024 under the Police Legislation Amendment Act 2024. Those laws define 'edged weapons' to include knives, machetes, sickles, scythes and axes, make carrying an edged weapon without lawful excuse an offence (max 3 years jail and $36,000 fine), bar sale/supply to under-18s, and empower metal-detector ('wanding') searches, with 'personal defence' explicitly excluded as a lawful excuse.

drugs_law_order.summary: Giddens announced (9 January 2025) a $14.5 million Cook Labor Government juvenile justice package targeting violent young offenders. Measures included trials for young repeat violent offenders within 28 days, immediate review of bail decisions for violent youth offenders, $1 million for tracking technology for offenders on bail, and penalties for responsible adults who fail to report bail curfew breaches. The package also funded a $13.5 million satellite Children's Court (former Armadale Court site) with an additional magistrate and a dedicated therapeutic court. Giddens said 'Every Western Australian has a right to feel safe in their own home,' describing the measures as 'common-sense reforms' delivering 'swifter outcomes for the victims and families of youth crime.'

gender_lgbtq.summary: Labor MLA (Kwinana); later WA Premier. As Health Minister, Cook publicly opposed gay conversion practices, stating on 3 July 2018: "I am strongly opposed to and do not support the use of sexual orientation change efforts of any kind," and describing practices that attempt to change sexual orientation as "abhorrent" and damaging to the mental health of LGBTI people. As Premier (Cook Labor Government) his government committed to banning LGBTIQA+ conversion practices and delivered gender recognition reforms abolishing the Gender Reassignment Board.

sources.title: WA Health Minister voices his opposition to gay conversion therapies
Western Australia To Ban Anti-Gay Conversion Practices

religious_freedom.summary: As Premier leading the Cook Labor Government, Cook's government committed to reforming and narrowing the religious exemptions under the Equal Opportunity Act 1984 (following the Law Reform Commission's 2022 review, which recommended limiting faith-based bodies' exemptions) and to strengthening Equal Opportunity protections for LGBTIQA+ staff and students in religious schools, including limiting when Christian schools can hire or enrol based on their beliefs. His government has also committed to introducing a conversion practices ban. The Index report noted the re-elected Cook Government's commitment to limiting faith-based schools' hiring and enrolment on the basis of belief. Before the March 2025 election, Cook declined to commit to specific timelines for these reforms, with responses delegated to the Attorney General.

sources.title: WA government targets 2025 for tackling religious exemptions to equal opportunity laws

He has also rejected claims linking liquor restrictions in the Kimberley to rising meth use.

Reading from prepared notes, Caddy confirmed the Government was aware of the Index and stated that 'the various Christian communities make valuable contributions to the lives of many Western Australians.' His response contained no specific pledge on Christian freedom of expression, committing instead to Equal Opportunity legislation that is 'fair and effective' and 'achieves a balance between the rights and interests of the diverse community living in our State.'

He is listed among current openly LGBTQ WA state politicians.

faith.summary: Christian. As leader of the Australian Christians and the party's first member elected to any Australian parliament, Groenewald is openly Christian. In her inaugural speech (19 June 2025) she said "the story of Jesus, his resurrection and the transforming truth of the gospel remains the biggest game changer in my life," while adding "I am not here to push my faith on anyone." She has spoken of her faith-based community work and praying weekly over her political work.

sources.title: Australian Christians Party Champions Christ and Christian Heritage in First Major Address
Speaker Announcement: Hon Maryka Groenewald MLC

gender_lgbtq.summary: Leader of the Australian Christians party and, after the 2025 state election, the first member of her party elected to any Australian state parliament (WA Legislative Council; inaugural speech 19 June 2025). The Australian Christians platform opposes same-sex marriage and homosexuality. Groenewald has made transgender health care a primary focus, repeatedly demanding the WA government publicly release the Child and Adolescent Gender Diversity Service's Standards of Care and accusing the government of "secrecy." Her amended transparency motion was defeated 19-17 in November 2025; she said it "has always been about ensuring that Parliament reaffirms its commitment to genuine transparency." She opposes gender-affirming care for minors. In a March 2026 Facebook post about a menopause health seminar she wrote: "A MAN cannot be a WOMAN."

sources.title: Maryka Groenewald
Australian Christians motion on transparency over gender treatment info fails
How media and MPs twist the news to attack transgender people

religious_freedom.summary: Australian Christians MLC and party leader, elected to the Legislative Council in 2025 as the first member of her party to win a state parliamentary seat. In her inaugural speech (19 June 2025) she championed Australia's 'Christian heritage' and argued that separation of church and state 'was always about preventing an exertion of the power of the state over the church, and vice versa' rather than limiting Christian expression in public. She listed party concerns including opposing 'moves to strip Christian schools of their freedom to employ staff who want to live out their faith,' preserving parental choice in education, protecting Christian teachers, and 'stopping aggressive activist LGBTQ+ legislation from making prayer a thought crime.' On 9 June 2026 she put a question without notice to the Legislative Council citing the Australian Christian Freedom Index (which ranked WA the 'least restrictive' jurisdiction in Australia), asking whether the Government would commit to protecting Christians' right to express their faith freely under any changes to the Equal Opportunity Act.

gender_lgbtq.summary: Liberal MLC and a leading social conservative in the WA Liberal Party. During the 2019 debate on gender reassignment/recognition legislation, Goiran argued gender dysphoria should be treated as a psychological matter, stating: "In essence there are only two alternatives. One perspective believes the solution is a physical one... Whereas others, including me, believe the solution should be to treat it psychologically." He raised concerns about young people accessing gender services at Perth Children's Hospital. In August 2013 he opposed same-sex marriage in parliamentary remarks (comments criticised by then-Premier Colin Barnett). In March 2023 he publicly supported Victorian Liberal MP Moira Deeming. In September 2024 he opposed the process used to pass the Births, Deaths and Marriages Registration Amendment (Sex or Gender Changes) Bill, calling the government's use of urgent legislation a "political battering ram." He is described as one of the staunchest pro-life campaigners in WA parliament.

religious_freedom.summary: Long-serving Liberal MLC (since 2009) and Leader of the Opposition in the Legislative Council since May 2025, also Shadow Attorney General. A self-described conservative Christian who attended Christian schools and served on the board of the Association for Christian Education. He sponsors a campaign titled 'Leave Faith-Based Schools Alone,' reflecting his support for protecting faith-based educational institutions. Historically he opposed same-sex marriage, speaking against it in parliament in August 2013, and is pro-life and opposed to euthanasia and surrogacy.

drugs_law_order.summary: A barrister and one of the WA Parliament's most prominent socially conservative members, Goiran has served as Shadow Attorney General (April 2021-February 2023, and again from December 2024) and is Leader of the Opposition in the Legislative Council. On law and order, in 2025 he vocally opposed Labor proposals to raise the age of criminal responsibility from 10 to 12 or 14, arguing against increased leniency for young offenders. He has a long record of justice-related advocacy, including a 2017 parliamentary motion for an inquiry into elder abuse seeking to make it a specific criminal offence.

gender_lgbtq.summary: WA Greens parliamentary leader and the party's LGBTQIA+ spokesperson. Pettitt has been a vocal advocate for banning conversion practices, reforming the Equal Opportunity Act, and gender recognition reform via a self-identification (statutory declaration) model. On the 2024 Births, Deaths and Marriages Registration Amendment (Sex or Gender Changes) Bill (which abolished the Gender Reassignment Board), he supported abolishing the board but opposed the bill as written, saying the government's handling was "undemocratic" and that the bill "continues to medicalise trans, intersex and gender-diverse identities," while calling the Gender Reassignment Board "a cruel, unnecessary, and humiliating barrier." In the November 2025 debate on Maryka Groenewald's gender-treatment transparency motion, he said "We want the LGBTQIA+ community to be granted equality and autonomy over medical processes," supporting trans youth access to care.

religious_freedom.summary: Greens MLC who has advocated for banning conversion practices in WA and for reforming the Equal Opportunity Act. Launching the Greens' LGBTQIA+ election platform priorities (12 February 2025), Pettitt said: 'In the next Parliament...the Greens (WA) will fight to ensure that life-changing reforms to the Equal Opportunity Act and the banning of archaic and harmful conversion practices...are finally realised.' He has criticised WA Labor's failure to deliver on promises to reform the Equal Opportunity Act, ban conversion practices and scrap the gender reassignment board, and stated the Greens have long pushed the government to reform the Act.

drugs_law_order.summary: Leader of the Legalise Cannabis WA Party and a GP, Walker is the WA Parliament's leading advocate for cannabis law reform. On 19 March 2026 he introduced the Misuse of Drugs (Lawful Personal Use of Cannabis) Amendment Bill 2026 in the Legislative Council, which would amend the Misuse of Drugs Act 1981 to allow adults to cultivate up to six cannabis plants at their primary residence, possess up to 50 grams for personal use, and gift up to 50 grams to other adults (no commercial sale, no public use, no use around children), and would void existing infringement notices for conduct made lawful. He framed it as 'treating adults like adults' and argued the decades-long prohibition is 'without merit' since medicinal cannabis was legalised in 2016. He became party leader in May 2024 and was re-elected in the 2025 state election.

sources.title: Cannabis Bill 2026
